name: Kelpie
description: The Kelpie is a shape-shifting water spirit inhabiting the lochs and pools of Scotland. It typically appears as a horse, often a beautiful and seemingly tame steed, tempting travelers to ride it. However, once mounted, the Kelpie's skin becomes adhesive, preventing the rider from dismounting. The Kelpie then plunges into the deepest part of the water, drowning and devouring its victim, leaving only entrails and occasionally the liver floating to the surface. While most stories depict the Kelpie as a malevolent entity, some tales suggest that they can be overcome or even tamed. They are often associated with storms and rising water levels, serving as a warning to stay away from dangerous waters. The Kelpie's allure stems from its ability to exploit human desires for beauty, power, and ease, ultimately leading to their demise. Its existence serves as a cautionary tale about the dangers lurking beneath the surface of seemingly innocent appearances.
subtitle: A shape-shifting water spirit of Scottish folklore.
appearance: Typically appears as a beautiful black horse, but can also take human form, often as a handsome man. When in equine form, its mane is often dripping wet, and its hooves may be reversed.
habitat: Lochs, rivers, and pools in Scotland.
behavior: Lures victims onto its back with the promise of a ride, then drags them to their watery grave. Can also cause floods and storms.

keyMyths.title: The Kelpie of Loch Ness
keyMyths.description: One story tells of a Kelpie inhabiting Loch Ness. It lured several children onto its back, then dived into the loch, drowning all but one. The surviving child managed to escape and warn others, leading to the Kelpie being driven away, but not before claiming several lives. This tale reinforces the Kelpie's predatory nature and association with dangerous waters. The association with Loch Ness adds to the mystique and fear surrounding the creature.
keyMyths.source: Folk Tales of the Scottish Highlands

keyMyths.title: Taming the Kelpie
keyMyths.description: A blacksmith is said to have captured a Kelpie by using a bridle with a silver cross. He then forced the Kelpie to work for him, hauling stones and performing other difficult tasks. The Kelpie was eventually released, but only after years of servitude. This story illustrates the possibility of overcoming a Kelpie, though at a great risk and with specific tools.

keyMyths.title: The Hair Rope
keyMyths.description: In some variations, a Kelpie can be identified by its perpetually wet mane. If a strand of its hair is braided into a rope, the rope becomes incredibly strong, but will also try to drag anyone holding it into the water. This highlights the Kelpie's inherent connection to water and the danger associated with even the smallest part of its being. The rope serves as a constant reminder of the Kelpie's power.
keyMyths.source: The Secret Commonwealth of Elves, Fauns and Fairies

sources.author: Briggs, Katharine
sources.work: An Encyclopedia of Fairies, Hobgoblins, Brownies, Bogies, and Other Supernatural Creatures
sources.description: Provides a comprehensive overview of fairies and other supernatural beings, including the Kelpie, within British folklore.
sources.text: Provides a comprehensive overview of fairies and other supernatural beings, including the Kelpie, within British folklore.

sources.author: MacKillop, James
sources.description: Offers detailed information on Celtic myths and legends, including the Kelpie's place within Scottish folklore and its connections to other water spirits.
sources.text: Offers detailed information on Celtic myths and legends, including the Kelpie's place within Scottish folklore and its connections to other water spirits.
